source: Derived from RDNA3, KSE Institute, EU4Reconstruction programme documents, EIB EU4Ukraine platform

Cherkasy Oblast.label: Solar + agricultural co-location
Cherkasy Oblast.rationale: Flat terrain and high solar irradiance (1,400+ kWh/m²/yr) suit agri-PV installations over existing farmland. Proximity to Kremenchuk Reservoir offers complementary hydro balancing.

Cherkasy Oblast.label: Prefabricated housing factory
Cherkasy Oblast.rationale: Central location and rail connectivity to conflict-affected eastern oblasts positions Cherkasy as a staging facility for rapid residential reconstruction supply chains.

Cherkasy Oblast.label: Chernozem soil-carbon programme
Cherkasy Oblast.rationale: Among Ukraine's richest chernozem soils; large-scale regenerative agriculture and soil-carbon sequestration could generate verifiable carbon credits for EU voluntary markets.

Chernihiv Oblast.label: Timber-frame modular construction cluster
Chernihiv Oblast.rationale: Polissia forestry resources and existing timber processing provide a natural feedstock for cross-laminated timber (CLT) and modular housing production serving post-war reconstruction demand.

Chernihiv Oblast.label: Cross-border logistics node (UA–Belarus corridor, future)
Chernihiv Oblast.rationale: Chernihiv sits on the rail and road corridor connecting Kyiv to Belarus and Russia — post-settlement this becomes a prime EU–East intermodal node. Early infrastructure investment captures first-mover advantage.

Chernihiv Oblast.label: Polissia reforestation and peatland restoration
Chernihiv Oblast.rationale: War damage to forest and peatland in northern Chernihiv creates reforestation opportunities eligible for REDD+ and EU carbon market credits, with co-benefits for flood control.

Chernivtsi Oblast.label: IT services campus (Chernivtsi tech hub)
Chernivtsi Oblast.rationale: Chernivtsi's university, EU border location, and growing tech workforce (Sigma Software, others) make it a credible secondary IT hub to Lviv for nearshore European delivery.

Chernivtsi Oblast.label: EU–Moldova–Romania logistics triangle
Chernivtsi Oblast.rationale: Tri-border position with Romania and Moldova, coupled with EU Solidarity Lanes investment, makes Chernivtsi a natural customs-clearance and cross-border warehousing hub.

Chernivtsi Oblast.label: Carpathian foothill wind and micro-hydro
Chernivtsi Oblast.rationale: Elevated terrain along the Carpathian foothills supports small-to-medium wind installations; mountain rivers offer run-of-river micro-hydro prospects supporting local energy independence.

Crimea (Temporarily Occupied).label: Black Sea solar corridor (post-liberation planning)
Crimea (Temporarily Occupied).rationale: Crimea has Ukraine's highest solar irradiance (1,600+ kWh/m²/yr). Pre-liberation planning for large-scale solar — including offshore wind in the Black Sea shelf — is investment-grade work that can proceed now.

Crimea (Temporarily Occupied).label: Black Sea port reconstruction (Sevastopol, Kerch)
Crimea (Temporarily Occupied).rationale: Liberated Crimean ports would form the southernmost anchor of Ukraine's Black Sea logistics network, serving EU grain export routes and potential LNG import infrastructure.

Crimea (Temporarily Occupied).label: Tourism and hospitality reconstruction
Crimea (Temporarily Occupied).rationale: Pre-war Crimea hosted 6M+ tourists annually. Post-liberation reconstruction of resort infrastructure, with EBRD/EIB concessional support, is a high-priority economic normalization investment.

Dnipropetrovsk Oblast.label: Green steel (DRI-EAF) at Kryvyi Rih cluster
Dnipropetrovsk Oblast.rationale: Kryvyi Rih holds one of Europe's largest iron ore deposits. Converting existing blast-furnace capacity to Direct Reduction Iron + Electric Arc Furnace with renewable hydrogen aligns with EU CBAM requirements and unlocks EU green-steel markets.

Dnipropetrovsk Oblast.label: Lithium and rare-earth extraction (Dnipro basin)
Dnipropetrovsk Oblast.rationale: Ukraine holds Europe's largest lithium reserves, with significant deposits in Dnipropetrovsk Oblast. EU Critical Raw Materials Act creates strong off-take demand; EBRD and EIB have active critical-minerals financing programmes.

Dnipropetrovsk Oblast.label: Defence-industrial cluster (Yuzhnoye/Pivdenmash ecosystem)
Dnipropetrovsk Oblast.rationale: The Pivdenmash rocket heritage and engineering talent base supports a scalable dual-use defence technology cluster — drones, precision systems, satellite components — with NATO and EU supply-chain contracts as primary off-take.

Donetsk Oblast.label: Rapid residential reconstruction factories
Donetsk Oblast.rationale: Donetsk Oblast's housing stock is the most severely damaged in Ukraine. Prefabricated modular construction facilities in liberated areas (Kramatorsk, Sloviansk) can deliver reconstruction units at 30–40% lower cost than traditional methods.

Donetsk Oblast.label: Post-war steel sector modernisation (Mariupol)
Donetsk Oblast.rationale: Azovstal and Illich facilities, once liberated, represent a greenfield-on-brownfield opportunity: rebuilding as green steel using EU CBAM-aligned processes, retaining Ukraine's steel export position with lower carbon intensity.

Donetsk Oblast.label: Energy independence for liberated communities
Donetsk Oblast.rationale: Decentralised solar microgrids for liberated communities provide energy resilience against continued infrastructure strikes while de-risking reconstruction investment through lower grid-dependency.

Ivano-Frankivsk Oblast.label: Carpathian wind corridor
Ivano-Frankivsk Oblast.rationale: The Carpathian ridgeline in Ivano-Frankivsk has measured wind speeds exceeding 7 m/s at hub height, sufficient for commercial-scale wind development. Proximity to Romanian and Slovak interconnectors enables export.

Ivano-Frankivsk Oblast.label: IT campus and nearshore delivery centre
Ivano-Frankivsk Oblast.rationale: A growing IT community (SoftServe, GlobalLogic presence) and EU border access position the oblast as a nearshore European tech delivery hub. Lower real-estate costs than Lviv are a draw for scale-up companies.

Ivano-Frankivsk Oblast.label: Carpathian forest carbon credits
Ivano-Frankivsk Oblast.rationale: Extensive Carpathian forestry under managed protection generates REDD+ and Article 6 carbon credits for EU voluntary buyers. Eco-tourism co-development adds revenue diversification.

Kharkiv Oblast.label: Defence-industrial tech cluster (Kharkiv)
Kharkiv Oblast.rationale: Kharkiv's FED enterprise, Morozov Design Bureau, and 14 higher-education institutions in engineering create a dense talent base for scaling drone, optronics, and electronic-warfare manufacturing aligned with NATO interoperability standards.

Kharkiv Oblast.label: IT and data centre cluster
Kharkiv Oblast.rationale: Ukraine's second-largest IT talent pool is in Kharkiv. Post-war reconstruction of campus infrastructure for tech multinationals, combined with national data-centre-resilience policy, supports a significant investment thesis.

Kharkiv Oblast.label: Steppe wind power (northern Kharkiv)
Kharkiv Oblast.rationale: The northern steppe zones of Kharkiv Oblast offer consistent Class 5–6 wind resources. Proximity to high-voltage transmission linking to the EU synchronisation zone (since March 2022) makes this a near-term export-oriented play.

Kherson Oblast.label: Kakhovka Reservoir wetland restoration
Kherson Oblast.rationale: The June 2023 dam collapse flooded 600 km² and destroyed unique steppe-wetland ecosystems. Verified wetland restoration generates Article 6 carbon credits while rebuilding biodiversity — an internationally supported nature-based solution.

Kherson Oblast.label: Southern solar corridor
Kherson Oblast.rationale: Kherson Oblast has the highest solar irradiance in mainland Ukraine. Once liberated and de-mined, large-scale agri-PV on former irrigated fields creates dual-use land value and energy export capacity.

Kherson Oblast.label: Kherson river port reconstruction
Kherson Oblast.rationale: Kherson port on the Dnipro provides inland waterway access to the Black Sea. Reconstruction as a multimodal grain and container terminal supports southern Ukraine's agricultural export corridor.

Khmelnytskyi Oblast.label: SMR deployment at Khmelnytska NPP site
Khmelnytskyi Oblast.rationale: Khmelnytska NPP's existing grid connection, cooling infrastructure, and licensed operators make it the leading site in Ukraine for Small Modular Reactor deployment — discussions with NuScale and GE-Hitachi are documented.

Khmelnytskyi Oblast.label: IDP housing prefab facility
Khmelnytskyi Oblast.rationale: Central location and rail access suit a large-scale prefabricated housing facility serving internally displaced populations across Podillia and southern Ukraine.

Khmelnytskyi Oblast.label: Podillia solar — grid complementing nuclear baseload
Khmelnytskyi Oblast.rationale: Solar capacity co-located near Khmelnytska grid infrastructure complements nuclear baseload output, reducing curtailment and improving overall system economics.

Kirovohrad Oblast.label: Uranium mining and nuclear fuel supply chain
Kirovohrad Oblast.rationale: Kirovohrad's Zhovta Richka and Myhiivske deposits feed the Smolino processing plant — expanding extraction capacity supports Ukraine's goal of domestic nuclear fuel production, reducing dependence on Westinghouse imports.

Kirovohrad Oblast.label: Central Ukraine solar — geographic centre advantage
Kirovohrad Oblast.rationale: The oblast's flat central-Ukrainian terrain and proximity to national grid backbone make it a cost-effective location for utility-scale solar complementing the eastern/southern industrial load centres.

Kirovohrad Oblast.label: Prefabricated construction — central distribution hub
Kirovohrad Oblast.rationale: Geographic centre of Ukraine provides equidistant rail distribution to all conflict-affected regions. A large-format modular housing plant here minimises last-mile logistics costs for nationwide reconstruction.

Kyiv City.label: Kyiv data centre and cloud resilience cluster
Kyiv City.rationale: Ukraine's digital-infrastructure policy prioritises domestic data sovereignty. Hardened Tier-3 data centres in Kyiv, with EU certification, serve both national e-government and private cloud demand from multinationals re-entering.

Kyiv City.label: Mixed-use urban reconstruction (war-damaged districts)
Kyiv City.rationale: Damaged Kyiv districts (Obolon, Troieshchyna) are redevelopment opportunities with established urban demand. EU-standard energy-efficient residential and commercial mixed-use projects attract both domestic and diaspora capital.

Kyiv City.label: Defence-tech startup ecosystem (Kyiv)
Kyiv City.rationale: Kyiv hosts Ukraine's largest tech talent concentration and is home to the leading drone and AI-defence startups (Saker, DroneUA, Molfar). Kyiv Innovation Hub model — combining EBRD venture facilities with NATO trust fund co-investment — is the template for the sector.

Kyiv Oblast.label: Bucha–Irpin reconstruction-era housing hub
Kyiv Oblast.rationale: The Kyiv Oblast corridor (Bucha, Irpin, Borodyanka, Hostomel) is the highest-profile residential reconstruction zone in Ukraine. Modular factory output co-located here dramatically reduces reconstruction timelines for the symbolic post-war rebuild.

Kyiv Oblast.label: Boryspil intermodal logistics expansion
Kyiv Oblast.rationale: Boryspil International Airport and the Kyiv–Warsaw rail corridor create a natural multimodal hub. Expansion of cargo capacity serves both humanitarian-aid and commercial logistics needs as Ukraine's international trade volumes recover.

Kyiv Oblast.label: Chornobyl Exclusion Zone solar (Solarplant project revival)
Kyiv Oblast.rationale: The 2 GW Chornobyl solar zone was partially developed before 2022. Reviving and expanding this project in the exclusion zone — where land is unusable for agriculture — converts a liability into a major renewable energy asset with existing grid connection.

Luhansk Oblast.label: Post-liberation housing factories (planning stage)
Luhansk Oblast.rationale: Luhansk Oblast will require massive residential reconstruction upon liberation. Pre-positioning prefab manufacturing capacity in adjacent free zones (Kharkiv, Dnipro) and planning reconstruction logistics now reduces post-liberation lag.

Luhansk Oblast.label: Steppe energy reconstruction (planning stage)
Luhansk Oblast.rationale: Western Luhansk's steppe terrain and existing HV grid infrastructure are assessed for post-liberation solar and wind development under Ukraine's EU Energy Community integration roadmap.

Luhansk Oblast.label: Alchevsk steel modernisation (post-liberation)
Luhansk Oblast.rationale: Alchevsk Metallurgical Plant, currently under occupation, is the primary post-liberation green-steel conversion opportunity in Luhansk — converting blast-furnace capacity to DRI-EAF aligned with EU CBAM from day one of liberation.

Lviv Oblast.label: Lviv IT campus (software export hub)
Lviv Oblast.rationale: Lviv is already Ukraine's largest IT hub outside Kyiv — home to SoftServe, EPAM, Intellias, and 100+ tech firms. Lviv IT Cluster has 30,000+ engineers. Campus investment supports scale-up to pan-European nearshore hub status.

Lviv Oblast.label: EU rail gauge integration (Lviv–Kraków corridor)
Lviv Oblast.rationale: Ukraine's broad-gauge rail meets EU standard gauge at the Polish border. Dual-gauge or gauge-change facilities in Lviv eliminate the single biggest bottleneck to rail-based trade with the EU — a EBRD priority investment under Ukraine's EU accession track.

Lviv Oblast.label: Western reconstruction staging hub
Lviv Oblast.rationale: Lviv's distance from the front and EU border connectivity make it the natural staging location for prefabricated building components manufactured in western Ukraine and shipped east. Integrates EU supply-chain partners.

Mykolaiv Oblast.label: Mykolaiv port reconstruction (Black Sea grain hub)
Mykolaiv Oblast.rationale: Mykolaiv was historically Ukraine's largest grain-export port complex before 2022 closures. Reconstruction with modern containerised facilities and EU-standard phytosanitary inspection positions it as the anchor of the southern export corridor.

Mykolaiv Oblast.label: Southern steppe wind (Black Sea corridor)
Mykolaiv Oblast.rationale: The coastal steppe zone has measured wind speeds exceeding 8 m/s — among the best onshore wind resources in Europe. Black Sea offshore wind feasibility studies (started by EBRD, halted by war) are investment-ready for post-stabilisation.

Mykolaiv Oblast.label: Shipbuilding-to-modular-construction conversion
Mykolaiv Oblast.rationale: Mykolaiv's shipbuilding industrial base (large-format steel fabrication, skilled welders, crane infrastructure) can be partially converted to modular-construction production for reconstruction, preserving jobs and industrial skills.

Odesa Oblast.label: Odesa/Chornomorsk port modernisation (Black Sea container hub)
Odesa Oblast.rationale: Odesa port complex is Ukraine's largest and the primary Black Sea access point. Post-war port modernisation — including automated container terminals, cold-chain facilities, and Black Sea Grain Initiative-aligned grain handling — is a tier-1 investment.

Odesa Oblast.label: Black Sea offshore wind (near-shore)
Odesa Oblast.rationale: The northwestern Black Sea shelf has shallow water depths (under 30m) suitable for fixed-foundation offshore wind. Ukraine's EU Energy Community membership and planned HVDC interconnection to Romania make this a near-term development opportunity.

Odesa Oblast.label: Odesa heritage-district reconstruction and tourism revival
Odesa Oblast.rationale: UNESCO-listed Odesa historic district was damaged by repeated missile strikes. Heritage reconstruction combining EU grants with hospitality real-estate investment supports the long-term tourism economy.

Poltava Oblast.label: Poltava gas-field renewable transition
Poltava Oblast.rationale: As the Shebelynka gas field depletes, renewable energy infrastructure on adjacent land reuses existing grid connections and skilled energy workers. A 'just transition' model with strong EU Facility eligibility.

Poltava Oblast.label: Central Ukraine construction supply chain
Poltava Oblast.rationale: Poltava's rail hub at Kremenchuk, combined with the Kremenchuk industrial complex, supports a large-format prefabricated construction cluster serving the eastern reconstruction zone.

Poltava Oblast.label: Poltava chernozem regenerative agriculture credits
Poltava Oblast.rationale: Rich Poltava chernozem supports one of Europe's highest soil-carbon sequestration potentials under regenerative agriculture. Verified credits under the EU's Carbon Removal Certification Framework command premium prices.

Rivne Oblast.label: Rivne NPP capacity expansion (AP1000 units 5–6)
Rivne Oblast.rationale: Rivne NPP has documented plans for two additional AP1000 units, with Westinghouse as partner. The existing licensed site, cooling infrastructure, and skilled workforce make this Ukraine's fastest-path nuclear capacity addition.

Rivne Oblast.label: Polissia amber and rare-earth processing
Rivne Oblast.rationale: Rivne holds Europe's largest amber deposits and associated heavy-mineral sands with rare-earth content. Value-add processing (cutting, polishing, rare-earth separation) rather than raw export creates sustainable regional revenue.

Rivne Oblast.label: Polissia peatland restoration
Rivne Oblast.rationale: Rivne's Polissia wetlands and peatlands are significant carbon sinks. Post-war drainage-reversal and peatland restoration generates REDD+ credits while reducing wildfire risk from dried peat.

Sumy Oblast.label: Post-war energy independence microgrids
Sumy Oblast.rationale: Sumy Oblast's proximity to the Russian border and documented cross-border shelling require energy-resilient decentralised microgrids for border communities. Solar + storage + local grid redesign is the recommended model.

Sumy Oblast.label: Border security technology cluster
Sumy Oblast.rationale: The oblast's 600 km border with Russia creates sustained demand for border-surveillance technology — sensors, radar, drone detection. Local engineering talent and proximity to Kharkiv's tech base support a specialised cluster.

Sumy Oblast.label: Border-community reconstruction housing
Sumy Oblast.rationale: Sumy border communities require rapid reconstruction once demined. Pre-positioned modular housing capacity addresses the acute IDP housing need in one of Ukraine's most shelling-affected non-frontline oblasts.

Ternopil Oblast.label: Podillia Upland wind
Ternopil Oblast.rationale: Ternopil's Podillia upland terrain channels wind reliably; measured resources support 150–200 MW of onshore wind with export potential via the Khmelnytska grid connection.

Ternopil Oblast.label: Western reconstruction supply — Galician hub
Ternopil Oblast.rationale: Ternopil provides a western-Ukraine supply-chain node for prefabricated construction materials, complementing Lviv's logistics role with lower land and labour costs.

Ternopil Oblast.label: Podillia agricultural carbon programme
Ternopil Oblast.rationale: Ternopil's agricultural land (grain, sugar beet) on moderate-chernozem soils is eligible for EU-certified regenerative-agriculture carbon credits under the Carbon Removal Certification Framework.

Vinnytsia Oblast.label: Vinnytsia IT campus (regional tech hub)
Vinnytsia Oblast.rationale: Vinnytsia IT Cluster has 5,000+ engineers and growing — lower costs than Kyiv/Lviv with good transport links. IT campus investment and co-working infrastructure supports scale-up to a mid-tier European nearshore hub.

Vinnytsia Oblast.label: Sugar-industry diversification into construction materials
Vinnytsia Oblast.rationale: Vinnytsia's large sugar-processing capacity experiences seasonal overcapacity. Diversification into bio-based construction materials (straw boards, hemp fibre insulation) using agricultural by-products is a documented circular-economy opportunity.

Vinnytsia Oblast.label: Podillia solar park
Vinnytsia Oblast.rationale: Flat agricultural land and a favourable solar resource (1,350 kWh/m²/yr) support large-scale utility solar. The 2022 DTEK Vinnytsia solar project precedent demonstrates bankability.

Volyn Oblast.label: Kovel–Chelm EU logistics corridor
Volyn Oblast.rationale: Volyn's rail connection to Poland at the Kovel–Chelm crossing is one of Ukraine's three main EU entry points. Expanding intermodal handling capacity reduces dwell times and unlocks higher trade volumes under EU Solidarity Lanes.

Volyn Oblast.label: Timber modular construction (Polissia forestry)
Volyn Oblast.rationale: Volyn's Polissia timber resources support a mass-timber and modular-construction cluster. Close proximity to Poland enables export of prefabricated building components into EU markets.

Volyn Oblast.label: Polissia forest and wetland restoration credits
Volyn Oblast.rationale: Volyn holds extensive Polissia wetlands and forests with REDD+ and peatland-restoration credit potential. Cross-border carbon programmes with Poland under Article 6 are under EU negotiation.

Zakarpattia Oblast.label: Four-border EU rail gauge integration hub
Zakarpattia Oblast.rationale: Zakarpattia borders four EU member states (Slovakia, Hungary, Romania) and Poland via Ukraine. The Chop rail gauge-change facility is the most-used EU–Ukraine rail crossing. Investment in dual-gauge tracks and intermodal handling transforms it into a major EU trade gateway.

Zakarpattia Oblast.label: Carpathian ridge wind and micro-hydro
Zakarpattia Oblast.rationale: The main Carpathian ridge in Zakarpattia has wind speeds above 7.5 m/s at 100m height. Mountain streams support 50+ MW of micro-hydro. Both technologies reduce the region's dependence on grid-supplied power.

Zakarpattia Oblast.label: Carpathian tourism and health resort reconstruction
Zakarpattia Oblast.rationale: Zakarpattia's Svalyava mineral water spa resorts and Bukovel proximity attract EU tourists. Post-war investment in EU-standard hospitality infrastructure (hotels, wellness centres, ski facilities) taps into growing central European health-tourism demand.

Zaporizhzhia Oblast.label: Zaporizhzhia NPP restoration (post-liberation, 6 units)
Zaporizhzhia Oblast.rationale: ZNPP is Europe's largest nuclear plant (6×1000MW). Post-liberation restoration to safe operation under IAEA monitoring is the single highest-impact energy investment in Ukraine. Westinghouse has documented readiness to support restoration.

Zaporizhzhia Oblast.label: Zaporizhstal green steel conversion
Zaporizhzhia Oblast.rationale: Zaporizhstal (Metinvest's Zaporizhzhia plant) is a primary candidate for DRI-EAF conversion, using the region's existing grid and potential hydrogen from ZNPP electrolysis. Aligns with EU CBAM from the earliest feasible post-liberation window.

Zaporizhzhia Oblast.label: Southern steppe solar (Azov Sea corridor)
Zaporizhzhia Oblast.rationale: The steppe zone between Zaporizhzhia city and the Azov Sea has documented 1,450 kWh/m²/yr solar irradiance. Large-scale utility solar in liberated areas provides interim power supply before ZNPP restoration is complete.

Zhytomyr Oblast.label: Granite and prefab construction cluster
Zhytomyr Oblast.rationale: Zhytomyr is Ukraine's leading granite-quarrying region. Combining granite aggregate with prefabricated construction production creates a vertically integrated construction-materials cluster with export capacity to the EU.

Zhytomyr Oblast.label: Korosten defence manufacturing cluster
Zhytomyr Oblast.rationale: Korosten's missile-component manufacturing plant is a foundation for a broader aerospace and defence electronics cluster. Proximity to Kyiv enables integration with the capital's defence-tech startup ecosystem.

Zhytomyr Oblast.label: Polissia solar and biomass energy
Zhytomyr Oblast.rationale: Zhytomyr's flat terrain south of the Polissia forest belt suits utility solar. Timber processing residues support biomass co-generation, providing renewable baseload that complements solar intermittency.
